title: A framework for low-overhead quantum fault tolerance via spacetime lifting
authors: Yijia Xu, Yixu Wang, Zi-Wen Liu
abstract: Fault-tolerant quantum computation is inherently a spacetime problem, requiring not merely good static quantum error-correcting codes but also low-overhead protocols for protecting and manipulating encoded quantum information over time. Fault complexes provide a homological framework for treating such protocols as single spacetime objects. In this work, we initiate the study of low-overhead fault complexes by introducing {spacetime lifting}, a method that constructs fault complexes from symmetry-reduced product structures beyond standard foliation. We show that spacetime lifting yields fault complexes and in particular {spacetime-lifted} memory experiments with almost-linear fault distance in the total spacetime cost, which substantially outperforms existing constructions. We further interpret fault complexes as measurement-based cluster-state protocols and identify general conditions under which they realize fault-tolerant logical teleportation, showing that spacetime-lifted constructions combine favorable scaling with operational schemes. Our study opens a path toward more efficient quantum fault tolerance through general complex constructions.
summary: This paper introduces 'spacetime lifting' to construct advanced fault complexes designed for robust quantum computation. It demonstrates that this method achieves near-optimal fault tolerance scaling with minimal overhead compared to traditional methods. This provides a general algebraic framework for designing highly efficient, spacetime-aware quantum error correction protocols.
main_problem: Developing low-overhead protocols for fault-tolerant quantum computation that account for both spatial and temporal overheads (spacetime).
main_result: The authors show that 'spacetime lifting' yields fault complexes whose fault distances scale almost linearly with the total spacetime cost, significantly outperforming existing constructions.
method: The work introduces 'spacetime lifting,' a method to construct fault complexes from symmetry-reduced product structures beyond standard foliation, and interprets these complexes as measurement-based cluster-state protocols.
model_or_system: The model uses algebraic formalism via 4-term chain complexes (fault complexes) derived from lifted product codes. These complexes describe spacetime fault-tolerant processes for quantum information encoding.
key_observables: Fault Distance ($d_{primal}, d_{dual}$), Spacetime Cost, Logical Teleportation success.
important_parameters: Almost linear scaling $d \ge \Omega(N/\log N)$ vs. polynomial scaling of older methods; expansion parameters $\alpha,  eta$.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the existence of product structures and the ability to construct fault complexes via spacetime lifting.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the comparison between standard foliation and spacetime lifting for achieving high fault distance with low overhead cost; diagrams show the structure of 4-term chain complexes mapping components to physical qubits/checks.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by defining the need for spacetime consideration, introducing the algebraic framework (fault complexes), developing the 'spacetime lifting' construction, demonstrating its superior scaling properties over existing methods, and finally applying it to realize logical teleportation protocols.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful, general algebraic tool—the fault complex—to analyze resource scaling in quantum computation that explicitly incorporates time evolution (spacetime), which is crucial for realistic hardware implementations beyond static error correction.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Comparison between (a) foliation and (b) spacetime lifting. In foliation, we take tensor product between a good lifted product code with a repetition code. The lifted product code has linear distance d, to achieve fault distance d, we have to let the repetition code to have at least length-d, so the total spacetime cost is O(d2). However, in spacetime lifting, we take a lifted product (tensor product quotiented out a ℤd cyclic symmetry) between a distance-d lifted product code and a thickened length-d repetition code with portal, to achieve fault distance being at least d, we only need O(d) spacetime cost instead of O(d2), which can also be interpreted as fault-tolerant syndrome...

FIG. 2. Foliated 2D toric code, where the black cycles represent primal faults associated with a X (primal) detector, and the green cycles represent dual faults associated with a Z (dual) detector. Here we consider the phenomenological error model.

FIG. 3. The third classical code C used in the double lifted product LP(A, B, C), where an edge represents a classical check, and a node represents a classical bit. (a) cyclic repetition code with parity check matrix 1 + x. The right figure represents the undetectable fault along the direction of 1 + x which is porportional to jℓwith weight being at least ℓ. (b) The ‘thickened’ the cyclic repetition code by introducing the top and bottom portal whose parity check matrix is C. The right figure represents a undetectable fault which includes non-trivial action along the cyclic repetition component 1 + x, hence the undetectable fault has weight at least 3ℓ.
